sentences.text: No food at all.
No water at all.
This ride was a horrible ride.
People were screaming.
People started to fight.
Everyone wanted to get near the little window to see what's go-- where they're going, where-- where we come.
And that started around 8 o'clock in the morning, 8:00 in the morning, until at night we arrived Warsaw.
Warsaw, we stood there.
And in the morning, about 8 o'clock, the train rolled from Warsaw out.
We can off our little stage.
Was hardly a few houses.
And they called by the name of Treblinka.
No.
No bathroom.
No bathroom.
Nothing.
Nobody has to guard us, because they was locked.
And on every car were two or four Germans with machine guns.
So nobody has to guard.
No.
We know.
We know.
The people coming from Poland, they know exactly where we're going.
I didn't believe.
I didn't believe.
I know that, from that train, nobody going to survive.
But I don't know.
I had, in my heart, a believing that I'm going to-- I'm going to make it.
No.
Not at all.
Nobody was talking to us.
I wasn't allowed to talk.
Even if somebody passed by the train, it was not allowed nearby.
Not at-- not in my car.
It was in the morning, about 8 o'clock.
It was September the-- September the 29-- the 30th, September.
No, no, no, no.
The door did not open of it.
No.
We stayed.
It was three additional trains waiting also.
One train left.
And each train consist-- I-- I know, because I worked in Treblinka-- of between 40 and 50 cars.
And every train had the minimum of 6,000 people.
So one train went in.
Then train came back empty.
The second train went in.
It came back [INAUDIBLE] also, also empty.
And then, the train I was in started to roll into Treblinka extermination camp.
Over there, when we got there, all of a sudden, the door opened with hollering from the Germans, Ukrainian, and the Jewish people that worked for them.
Like they took us out with the blue bands.
There was certain companies, like the red and the blue.
There was, like I would say, chosen people, the one which they were chosen to wear the blue armbands.
They could live a little longer.
Their job was to take out the people from the train and to take them to the main undressing place.
Was outside, not inside.
Beside the women, and the small children were lead to the barrack, very large barrack.
And the woman and the children, they undressed over there.
But the men, they undressed themself all naked.
And they were waiting in groups to be let in to the gas chambers.
When the door opened up and they were stopped hollering, men on the right, women to the barracks, well, naturally, I went with the men.
And my little brother went with me, which I couldn't help him too much.
And my [INAUDIBLE]-- I never saw them again, or my mother.
No.
No.
No.
All of a sudden, they opened up the door.
We had no chance to even to say goodbye to each other.
And when we went to that place, very big place, we undressed ourself.
I always thought-- there came an order to take out a few people, about six, seven people.
And one of mine relative, he knew a man from Warsaw which was wearing the red band.
The red band was very high society, accordingly, because they had a chance to live better, to eat better, and to live longer there.
They-- what they did, they prepared the people to the gas chamber.
They [INAUDIBLE] them.
They give them orders how to undress, what to do with their clothes, what to do with their shoes, with the-- with the belonging, like gold, silver, other thing.
That was their job.
So one of my relative know this guy.
And he said, come out.
And they took out a cousin of mine, myself, and two more that they did took out.
From 18,000 people, they took out five people.
And I was-- and I was in that group from the five people.
And they-- oh, they asked to stay on the side, until they finished up with the whole group.
And from this day, we were in Treblinka in the working place, working commando.
Can you describe the physical layout of Treblinka where you were?
Physical layout in Treblinka is you went in with the railroad train.
You got out from the railroad train of the undressing place.
One side was a barrack.
On the other side, was a barrack where the-- the women undressed themself.
In the middle, there was a place where they took water from there, some to drink, some of them not to.
Then, it was, from there, there was a way, which they called [NON-ENGLISH].
That means 'The Road to the Heaven,' going into the gas chamber.
On the right side, there was a place where people worked.
We worked over there taking all those clothes to big, big places, like shoes, or clothes, six, seven, story high of clothes.
And we had to put together cotton with cotton, silks with silk, and-- and the same thing with shoes.
Yes.
That's-- most of us, we worked over there, besides some people that worked in the kitchen, or some people who-- that worked in other places.
But too many people never was in the workings commando from Treblinka.
The top number reached up to 800 was allowed.
Well, there's not too much to tell about, because those people were already undressed and going into the gas chamber, which [INAUDIBLE], nobody could go, because nobody ever returned that road.

What I found out is, when I was assigned as a barber to cut the woman's hair in the gas chamber-- that was the beginning, then we did cut women's hair in the gas chamber-- then, I find out what it is and how it was.
Yes, from both side, from both side enclose-- not only what it was enclosed, it wasn't enclosed by buildings, but trees from both sides, which nobody could escape from this side, though.
There was no place where it was escaped.
And beside that, there were the Germans.
And there were-- there were the Ukrainians holding their post around.
So people, they could not-- they had to go in, direct to the gas chamber.
The physical layout of the gas chambers.
No.
No.
There was entirely a different-- not only barrack, different place.
Nobody but nobody could go near from the place-- they call this the larger-- one to number two, because the number two was actually the death lager.
That's what they called it, because, over there, the job is only by killing people.
Whoever went over there from the lager number one could never return back to lager number one.
Who went to lager number two could never return it.
It wasn't far.
I would say about 60, 70 meter.
Not far at all.
No, there wasn't a number.
No.
It was-- it was more than one.
It was four or five of them.
Yes.
Well, I dressed.
They told me to get dressed and to wait.
The clothes what I had.
Yeah, same clothes.
Over there, there wasn't any different clothes, but only the clothes what you had.
There wasn't clothes, like in any other concentration camp.
Like Auschwitz had special-- special clothes.
Yeah.
Well, assigned to do the work on the other side of the-- of the other side lager where they took the people in to do my job at the [SPEAKING GERMAN] we called it.
That mean select at the clothes in bundles, to take the bundles, take them to the railroad, pack him in the-- in the cars.
And they send away the clothes [INAUDIBLE].
Well, the condition-- we were sleeping on the floor.
There wasn't-- there wasn't any floor, it was just on the ground.
Inside my barrack wasn't too many.
Would say it was, maybe, 100 to 120 of them.
The leader inside-- in our barracks slept also commandant, the Jewish commandant from the Treblinka.
Galewski, ?] [INAUDIBLE] [?
Galewski. ?]
He was the Jewish commander.
I knew him when I was there, not personally, but just when I got there.
Well, it is accordingly.
To some people, he was very nice.
Some people, he was mean.
I don't mean mean that he killed somebody.
No, he-- I never saw him killing somebody.
But whipping?
Yes, I did see him.
Without reason, without reason, what this I saw him.
To my knowledge, he was a very nice, intelligent man.
He did whatever it was in his power not to hurt people, to help them.
No.
No.
My brother went in just a minute.
Came off this-- the train, undressed, and went into the gas chamber.
Yes, I was with a cousin of mine.
A day like?
It was like, in the morning, we had an appeal on the appeal place.
Well, that means we have to stay like soldiers.
They counted how many we-- people we are.
And from there, we went to the kitchen to have coffee and something else to the coffee.
With the-- sometime, we had bread.
Sometime, we had nothing.
And from there, we went out to the big place to do our job-- selection of the clothes.
Yes.
Yes.
Anytime, you could.
Yes, which I did.
Yes.
To bundle up, to take out from the clothes everything what people left inside.
And we find a lot of thing, a lot of gold, silver, diamonds, and money.
And what did you do with that?
In the clothes.
Well, we had to give that up to the Germans, which there's a valise, big valise, and we'd throw that in.
But we had plans of our own to escape.
And to escape, we need money, lot of money.
And we're hiding money too, between the bundles.
There wasn't another place, but for-- between the bundles.
Sometime, we had a package of money or other goods.
And-- and the following day, an hour later, they changed places with this one.
Somebody went over.
So you-- your bundle is not there anymore.
We worked sometime 12, 14 hours.
And sometime, we worked 18 hours.
It was when a transport came at night, we had to stay at night.
Oh.
They-- it came between two and four transport.
That means between 12,000 and 24,000 Jewish people were killed in one day.
We know in our part of the camp, because during the time when we work, we could communicate.
Outside, let's say at night, we couldn't go out from the barrack, even if you have to go out emergency.
It was not only locked, there was Germans outside, Ukrainian, and also with machine guns.
So nobody went near the door.
Well, only in daytime when we worked.
Well, working, you meet people that worked away from you, which they lived in a different barrack.
So that's the way we communicate.
Yeah.
With them, we couldn't communicate at all, not at all, because that was a different country, far away, like, let's say from here to Australia.
That's what I would say for us, because it was very, near, very, very near, only from the, I'd say, a couple of hundred meters from the barracks.
From where we work, it was about 50, 60 meters.
But from the barracks, and it was far away.
We had no communication whatsoever.
Yes.
Yes.
People were picked out, for any kind of reason, from-- mostly from the camp number one, which sometime, this-- this one doesn't do the right thing, you go over there.
Somebody went there, forget about it.
There was that.
In my camp, there was no women.
There was two women, which I found out about it.
They were over there.
They were working over there.
After the war, I know-- I know what-- one of them, Sonia.
I knew her very well.
I met her.
We were together in Israel.
A lovely woman.
But beside those two women, I don't know any other woman was there.
It could be, but I don't know.
Oh, when I were there, it was [?
Spangle. ?]
Yes.
No.
No.
Yeah.
His assistant was-- we called him [?
Kiever. ?] What is-- what was his name?
I cannot recall the name, but he had a German name.
I don't-- I don't recall exactly, but we called him the nickname, [?
Kiever. ?]
He was a horrible man.
He was a killer.
But according to the [?
Lalka, ?] he was an angel.
Well, [?
Lalka, ?] that [INAUDIBLE], which we call him the [?
Lalka. ?] He was a handsome man.
Lalka ?] means doll.
He was assistant, also, to the commandant.
And I would say even the Germans were afraid of.
The Germans working over there were afraid of.
He were very rough.
When he came out, especially with his dog, there wasn't a 15 minute that they didn't kill somebody.
Every 10, 15 minutes, somebody else got killed.
Just for sportsmanship, he did it.
Huh?
Yes.
Yes, many times.
Yes, I saw when he killed.
Yes, one time I had a conversation with him.
I was assigned to a barrack where it was packed with clothes.
And we selected those clothes, just like [?
Auschwitz. ?] And there was a group of people from a little town by the name of [?
Yenjeouf. ?] And the head kapo, Benjamin-- Benjamin [?
Kavolski-- ?] [?
Rockoffski-- ?] Benjamin [?
Rockoffski, ?] yes.
He came over to me and said, Abe, I got my brother, and I got some relatives from the same town I came from, and they're going to escape tonight.
I want you to bring in people you know from-- because there were a number of people from my home town.
With each transport, they came.
So we took out a few people.
Take out 11 men and let them work here in that barrack.
And somebody's going to ask you.
Everything is not-- all the people is working what they worked yesterday.

Well, I helped this organize the escape from his brother and from some more people. 11 of them, they escaped by crawling over the fences, that little leaves and grass which was around the barbed wire was taking off [INAUDIBLE] by those people.
And he came in, [?
Lalka, ?] [INAUDIBLE].
He asked, is anybody missing here?
So I answered, no, the all what worked yesterday, they're here, which was not the same.
Then, I was [INAUDIBLE].
And he came over to me.
He got me by my head-- I had a head of hair-- and he started knocking on a iron pipe, which I don't know how I didn't feel anything.
And that was the first time that the [?
Lalka ?] touched anybody and left them alive, that time I got with him.
Why do you think he let you go?
I don't know.
I have no idea.
I have no idea why I'm still alive, after going that thing through.
No, they were not caught.
What had happened, I find out after I escaped from Treblinka.
One of them came to me, to my home town.
He told me that, when they got back, 10 of them got killed by the Poles.
And he himself escaped.
And I met him also in Israel.
His name was Leibish Placefski.
Uprising.
Yeah, there was.
Yeah, there was.
The main, I would say, was [INAUDIBLE] [?
Dr.
Horozitski. ?]
He was a highly educated man, not only with doctor degree, but was very famous also in the Army.
And he was the leader to prepared one uprising.
From Warsaw.
No.
I know him because, at one time, I got very sick.
And [?
Kiever ?] told me to go to see him.
I don't know why.
The same thing.
Whenever I got very sick, I had to do with mine inside-- how you call this?
I had an attack of mine-- oh, how you call it?
Kidney.
I had a kidney attack.
OK.
